title: Filler Words
description: The glue words that make Hindi sound real. Textbooks ignore these — Delhi Hindi is built on them.
culturalNote: Filler words are the difference between sounding robotic and sounding fluent. In Delhi, 'matlab', 'waise', 'toh', and 'na' are constant. Once you hear them, you can't unhear them. Don't be afraid to use them — overuse is fine when learning.

exercises.english: I mean / Like / That is / So
exercises.note: Used exactly like 'I mean' in English. 'Matlab woh aaya hi nahi' = I mean, he just didn't show up. 'Matlab kya?' = What does that mean? / What do you mean? Both literal and filler uses.

exercises.english: By the way / Anyway / Like / Actually
exercises.spanish: Por cierto / De todas formas / La verdad es que
exercises.note: 'Waise bhi' = anyway / besides / in any case. 'Waise' at sentence start = by the way. 'Waise toh' = otherwise though. Incredibly common in Delhi speech — listen for it everywhere.
'Toh' is the Hindi 'so' — connects thoughts. 'Woh nahi aaya toh hum chale gaye' = He didn't come, so we left. 'Toh phir?' = So then? / What happened after? Very frequent connector.
When 'na' ends a sentence, it's a softener or tag question. 'Acha hai na?' = it's nice, right? 'Aana hai na tujhe?' = you're coming, right? It invites agreement and softens requests enormously.

exercises.prompt: 'Bahut acha tha na?' — what does this mean?
exercises.hint: 'Na' at the end = seeking agreement / confirmation

exercises.note: Tone is everything: fast dismissive 'haan haan' = yeah yeah I know, stop. Warm enthusiastic 'haan haan!' = yes yes absolutely! The speed and energy completely change the meaning.

exercises.prompt: Someone is explaining something you already know and you want them to stop — you say 'haan haan' in what tone?
exercises.hint: You're cutting them off, not agreeing enthusiastically

exercises.english: So then / And then? / What happened next?
exercises.note: 'Toh phir kya hua?' = So then what happened? Used to push a story forward or show you're engaged. 'Toh phir?' alone = so? / and then what? Good conversation signal.

exercises.english: Get to the point / Just say it directly
exercises.note: 'Seedha' = straight/direct. 'Seedha baat karo yaar' = just get to the point, man. Friendly frustration. You can also say 'seedha bol' = just say it straight.

exercises.sentenceEnglish: Man, don't beat around the bush, _____.

exercises.note: 'Main bhi' = me too. 'Woh bhi nahi aaya' = he also didn't come. 'Waise bhi' = anyway/besides. 'Bhi' attaches to words to mean 'even this one too'. Very short but very useful.
Not the English greeting! 'Woh hi nahi aaya' = he specifically didn't come (nobody else — HE didn't come). 'Yahi chahiye' = exactly this is what's needed. Adds emphasis and specificity.

exercises.prompt: 'Yahi chahiye tha' — what does 'hi' do in this sentence?
exercises.correctAnswer: Emphasises — this is EXACTLY what was needed
exercises.hint: 'Hi' in Hindi = emphasis particle, not a greeting

exercises.note: 'Kyunki' = because (for giving a reason). 'Main nahi aayi kyunki mujhe kaam tha' = I didn't come because I had work. Often dropped in very casual speech when the reason is obvious.
'Lekin' = but. 'Sab theek hai, lekin thaka hua hoon' = everything's fine, but I'm tired. In very casual speech, sometimes shortened to 'lik' or replaced with English 'but'.

exercises.instruction: Match the filler word to its role

exercises.note: 'Phir bhi' acknowledges something but overrides it. 'Bahut busy hoon, phir bhi aayi' = I'm very busy, but I came anyway. Shows effort, resilience, or contradiction.

exercises.sentence: Pata hai bahut kaam hai, _____ milne aana chahiye tha.
exercises.sentenceEnglish: I know there's a lot of work, _____ you should have come to meet.

exercises.prompt: You're telling a story. Your friend is listening and wants to hear what happened next. What do they say?

exercises.instruction: Match the phrase to its function
